data.title: Bank OZK: Why I Doubled My Position In The Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

In an article that was published earlier this year I argued that both the common shares and the preferred shares issued by Bank OZK (OZK) were attractive. I am somewhat surprised to see that the preferred shares have lost approximately 10% in value since that article was published, and I think this is an excellent opportunity to add to my position. In this article I will explain why I remain confident the bank will continue to perform quite well and why I'm gladly taking the 7%+ yield on the preferred shares despite the relatively high long-term interest rates.

I’m still not too worried about the CRE portfolio

I wasn't surprised to see that the bank’s net interest income remained strong. Compared to the third quarter of 2024, Bank OZK reported a 2% increase in its interest income, while the total amount of interest expenses decreased by almost 4%. This allowed the net interest income to increase by in excess of 6% to just under $415M USD.

As the income statement above also shows, the bank still relies very much on its net interest income, as the total amount of income that's not related to the net interest performance remained very limited to approximately $36 million, which of course was not sufficient to cover the $159 million in non-interest expenses.

This resulted in a pre-tax and pre-provision income of approximately $290 million USD, which is roughly 3% higher than in the same quarter last year. After also deducting the $48 million in loan loss provisions as well as the almost $58 million in taxes, the net profit came in at just under $185 million, of which the bank needed just over $4M to cover the preferred dividends.

This means that the net profit that was attributable to the common shareholders of Bank OZK came in at $180.5M, for an EPS of $1.60. This also pushed the total earnings per share in the first nine months of the current financial year to $4.66, despite recording in excess of $122 million in loan provisions in those first three quarters.

As expected, the bank still needs just a small portion of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends, so as a preferred shareholder, I’m fine with that.

The main concern of Mr. Market with Bank OZK is its substantial portfolio of commercial real estate. But as you can see below, the total amount of loans that are classified as "past due" is lower than at the end of Q2 2025 and lower than at the end of FY 2024.

And keep in mind the bank generated a pre-tax and pre-provision income of almost $830M in the first nine months of the current financial year, so it can handle a much higher loan loss provision rate without jeopardizing the preferred dividends.

And as always, the bank provides a transparent overview of the assets it currently has put in foreclosure. As you can see below, the foreclosed assets will likely be realized without any (meaningful) loss to Bank OZK.

I’m also happy with the recent LTV update; the bank re-appraised almost $3B in real estate during the third quarter, and as you can see below, while there are some LTV increases that warrant additional attention, there are also some loans where the LTV ratio decreased.

The preferred shares offer a good risk/reward ratio

As mentioned in the previous article, Bank OZK has just one series of preferred shares outstanding, and it timed the original issue of these preferred shares quite well. Trading with (OZKAP) as its ticker symbol, the fixed preferred dividend yield is just 4.625% based on the $25 principal value of the preferred shares. These preferred shares were issued in October 2021, when interest rates on the financial markets were very low.

As you can see below, the preferred shares closed at $15.95 on Tuesday, which means the current yield is approximately 7.25%.

Considering the robust financial results of Bank OZK, and considering the bank only needs a low single-digit percentage of its net income to cover the preferred dividends, I think the risk/reward ratio is very interesting down here, despite this series of preferred capital being non-cumulative.

Investment thesis

I already had a long position in OZKAP before, and earlier this week, I doubled the size of my position on the weakness in the share price. I currently have no position in the common shares of Bank OZK as all the put options that I have written expired out of the money, but I am writing additional put options as I would really like to establish a long position in Bank OZK. I’m aware of the relatively high exposure to commercial real estate, but as almost the entire CRE portfolio has been reassessed in the past 2-3 years, I trust the bank has identified its key risks and is recording an appropriate amount of provisions.

data.title: FOMC Minutes, Jobless Claims Report In Focus As 2025 Winds Down
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

U.S. stock index futures were little changed on Friday ahead of what is expected to be a trading session with low volumes post the Christmas holiday. Precious metals silver, gold, and platinum scaled new all-time highs, extending their massive run this year.

Despite the holiday-shortened week, with markets shut on Thursday for New Year's Day, investors will have several economic data releases to watch out for in the final week of the year. The highlights will be the Crude oil inventories data releasing on Monday, the FOMC releasing its minutes of the meeting on Tuesday, and initial jobless claims and Chicago PMI for December scheduled for Wednesday. S&P Global Manufacturing PMI for December is due on Friday.

Coffee Holding (JVA), 1933 Industries (TGIFF), Park Aerospace (PKE), Lifecore Biomedical (LFCR), Taylor Devices (TAYD), and Hurco (HURC) will report earnings next week. ___________________________________________________________________

Earnings Spotlight: Wednesday: Coffee Holding (JVA), 1933 Industries (TGIFF). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings Spotlight: Thursday: Park Aerospace (PKE), Lifecore Biomedical (LFCR). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings Spotlight: Friday: Taylor Devices (TAYD), Hurco (HURC). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility Watch: Energy Fuels (UUUU) and Applied Digital (APLD) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Hycroft Mining Holding (HYMC), Amicus (FOLD), and Charlotte's Web (CWBHF). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include CVR Energy (CVI), Tile Shop (TTSH), and Lamb Weston (LW).

Data Watch: Key data reports are due to arrive during the first few days of the month, including updates on deliveries from Tesla (TSLA), NIO (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Li Auto (LI), ZEEKR Intelligent (GELYF), Polestar Automotive (PSNY), and Lucid Group (LCID). Quarterly sales reports from General Motors (GM), Ford Motor (F), and Toyota (TM) will also be closely watched. Other reports of high interest to investors will be the monthly Macau gross gaming revenue release, the monthly update on Class 8 truck orders, firearm background check data from the FBI, and discount broker DARTs reports.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Be The Bank: I Am Locking In 7% Yields From Corporate And Municipal Borrowers Now
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

The economy is fueled by borrowing and lending. It is simply a reality of the modern economy that for individuals, companies, and even governments, borrowing is an efficient means of achieving goals more quickly.

We often talk about the downsides of debt. We talk about how people can get themselves in a financial hole where they find themselves on the debt treadmill, where new debt is accumulating faster than they can pay off the old debt.

Yet, the reality is that few people can buy a decent car, let alone a house, without taking on debt. And without a car, getting to a job to generate income is a challenge in many areas. For many, the cash demands of a mortgage are lower than what they would pay to rent a similarly sized space. Yes, people can be irresponsible with debt and put themselves in undesirable situations. Yet, the ability to get a loan for a car, a house, or to fill a cash need can help us achieve our life goals.

Companies aren't any different. It is very hard for a company to be competitive when all its capital is tied up in something that is essential, like real estate. The money that is tied up can often result in much higher earnings when it is freed up to invest in growing their business. REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts) often participate in "sale-leasebacks", where they buy real estate and lease it back to the seller. The REIT then collects "rent" and owns the property, while the seller has a lump sum of cash that is freed up to invest in growing their business while maintaining the right to continue using the real estate.

Governments borrow too. While it is often a contentious topic how much governments should be allowed to borrow, the reality is that cities and states frequently invest in projects that cost much more than their annual budget. Building water systems, schools, roads, and other infrastructure requires a lot of money up front for a result that will be used for decades.

Today, we are going to look at two investments where you can benefit from the realities of debt.

Pick #1: EPR – Yield 7.1%

EPR Properties (EPR) is a REIT that focuses on "experiential" real estate. Originally a REIT that solely owned AMC movie theaters, EPR has since diversified to other operators and other sectors. Today, it owns a portfolio of Topgolf (MODG) locations, water parks, amusement parks, museums, and more. Source.

EPR has been looking to diversify away from theaters, an effort that slowed down significantly during the COVID pandemic and the past few years of high interest rates. EPR has been patient, choosing to maintain a conservative balance sheet and wait for the time to be right to expand.

At the Q3 earnings call, management guided for $400-$500 million investment spending in 2026. That is more than double their investment spending in 2025.

EPR is closing out 2025 at the high-end of their acquisition guidance, recently disclosing the purchase of a 5-property golf portfolio in Dallas and Ocean Breeze Waterpark in Virginia Beach. The transactions are at a blended cap-rate of 8.6%.

An investor could have a lot of fun doing "due diligence" on EPR by traveling the country, visiting their locations! I've been to several myself, and knowing that you are raking in monthly dividends while you are enjoying the amenities is the definition of bliss.

EPR is turning in a solid 2025, and we expect 2026 to be even better. We also expect another dividend raise in March 2026, and with a more aggressive investment schedule, we could see the dividend growth rate accelerate in 2027 and beyond.

Pick #2: RMM – Yield 8%

RiverNorth Managed Duration Municipal Income Fund (RMM) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that invests in municipal bonds and in municipal bond funds. Since municipal bond funds are often leveraged, and RMM also uses leverage, the result is a fund that tends to amplify the returns of municipal bonds for better or worse.

2025 has been a relatively slow year for municipal bonds. These funds came into the year with a lot of optimism, then faltered during the April tariff drama and have only partially recovered, even as the Fed has cut rates several times.

This has people asking questions like: How come muni bonds are stagnant while interest rates are declining? Aren't declining rates good for municipal bonds?

The confusion comes with a somewhat inaccurate use of the term "interest rates." There is more than one interest rate. The interest rate that the Federal Reserve exerts control over is the "overnight rate," which is the lending market in which banks borrow or lend for very short-term loans. So, when the Fed is "cutting rates," they are actually cutting only one rate. The impact on any rates beyond the overnight rate is indirect and a result of the fact that institutions have a choice between one loan for X-days or a new loan every day for X days.

The shorter the term of a loan, the more impact the Federal Reserve's target rate decision will have. So when the Fed cuts rates, it has an immediate impact on overnight loans, it has a significant impact on 1-month loans, a little less on 1-year loans, and by the time you get out 5 or 10 years, it is perfectly possible that the interest rate on loans goes in a different direction.

We can see that since the Fed started cutting in late 2024, the 10-year Treasury (US10Y) is roughly in the same place as it was in July 2024, while the 30-year Treasury rate (US30Y) is actually higher:

Municipal bonds are very long-term bonds. They often have initial terms in excess of 30 years. As a result, municipal bond prices are going to primarily be influenced by the long-end of the interest rate curve. Lower short-term rates are still a positive for CEFs that use leverage, since they tend to borrow money at short-term interest rates. However, we have yet to see the recovery in the prices of the underlying bonds that would drive NAV higher. This is because the 30-year Treasury is still close to long-term highs.

As a result, we haven't seen the recovery of RMM's NAV yet.

We continue to believe that municipal bonds are trading at fantastic long-term prices. We will continue taking advantage of this buying opportunity for municipal bonds. RMM is a good option because it owns a high-quality portfolio of municipal bonds, while also having a 33% allocation to other CEFs that are trading at a discount to NAV. That strategy will provide outsized upside when municipal bond prices go up. Source.

Note that RMM's dividend policy is variable. Each year, the distribution resets at 7% of the prior year's NAV. Since NAV is a bit lower in 2025, we expect the distribution in 2026 will be lower than 2025 unless there is a late rally in municipal bonds. At the current NAV, the 2026 payout will be around $1.06, down 2.4% compared to 1.086 for 2025.

Conclusion

There are two types of people in this world. Those who collect interest, and those who pay interest. While it is likely that you have paid your share of interest over the years, as retirement approaches and you grow your nest egg, you should be transitioning into someone who collects interest.

Today, we looked at two options that provide everyday financing solutions for businesses and governments. EPR technically collects "rent," in deals that are structured where they own the real estate, but ultimately for the tenant, it is a way to free up a lump sum of capital and pay a predetermined fee over several decades.

RMM buys up municipal bonds and other funds that invest in municipal bonds– helping provide capital to the system that allows cities across the U.S. borrow the money they need for their capital spending.

Shareholders like you get to collect dividends from these companies. This provides you with the cash flow you need to cover your expenses, and with a bit left over to reinvest and grow your capital base even more.

Make it your goal to collect dividends, rather than pay interest!

data.title: Nvidia's Groq Megadeal; A $20B Inference Pivot To Stay King
data.description:
Nvidia: Sending A Message With The Groq Licensing Deal

Never go against Jensen Huang. I've learned it the hard way when I used to trade in and out of NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) stock. However, if I have stayed on board and not worry too much about whether Nvidia could shake off the doubts on its leadership, I might have been more profitable in my Nvidia position.

There are no shortages of rivals who want to snag market share from Nvidia. However, that doesn't mean that these companies can simply bore a hole through the Nvidia armor. It has always found a way to refute the market's pessimism.

And this time around, the masterstroke is about an up and coming rival that could have ramifications on Nvidia's long term leadership. In come the deal that was struck just before Christmas with Groq. I believe Jensen Huang thinks the deal should help not only to significantly improve Nvidia's inferencing opportunity, but also lower the chances of getting disrupted by Groq's ascendency.

It's also about "handpicking" the talents from this AI chip startup that is helmed by executives that were part of the founding team that developed Google's reputable TPUs, now already in v7 (Ironwood). In my last Nvidia writeup, I noted that the stock has now dropped to highly attractive levels, suggesting that the market appears to have turned too downcast. My view was also corroborated by Wall Street, as they highlighted that such pessimistic multiples in NVDA also proved to be opportune buy points previously, affording the investor who dared to pick the falling knife in betting on a subsequent revival in the stock.

I guess I don't have to remind the keen followers of Nvidia that the reported $20B non-exclusive licensing deal was inked between AI chip startup Groq and Nvidia. It was also a deal that appears to have been proposed and consummated quickly, since Groq reportedly wasn't looking for a sale. To be clear, I was also astounded by how quickly the deal panned out, and Nvidia probably recognized that the threats have intensified over the horizon.

It isn't just Google chipping at Nvidia's heels, but the notion that inference, could herald a "rapid shift towards inference, (which) could require more specialized chips." That plays distinctly into Groq's strengths given their specialized chips called Language Processing Unit or LPU, "and they are designed for fast and efficient inference." So, yeah. It's not just a big deal, but a massive one for the ecosystem and how the underlying changes afoot in the training/inference realm from 2026. And, with Groq's LPU being integrated into Nvidia's full stack, I think it clearly represents a big shoutout intended for its custom chips peers that Nvidia moves like "lightning" and Jensen Huang isn't going to be left stranded with the AI inferencing developments that we have observed recently.

So, I believe the pace of the deal could have taken rivals off-guard, since the deal was announced just before the Christmas Day celebration, as Jensen Huang wastes no time to bring on board some of the top talents for what is after all, a highly regarded AI startup that could be chipping at Nvidia's heels sometime down the road.

We have already seen AI startups from China gaining speed in seeking to dethrone the King of AI in China, given the snafu that has seen Nvidia's market share being throttled, and momentum stuttered to a standstill. While the encouraging approval for Nvidia's H200 chips could see the resumption of exports to China in the first half of 2026, the initial cadence will likely be scrutinized, although figures amounting up to 80K chips have been quoted.

In addition, it seems like that China's top AI and big tech companies could seek to leverage their clout and market access to Nvidia's H200 production and availability to make sure they get their hands on them as early as possible. Hence, I think upside optionality on Nvidia's China sales will finally see some constructive operating results before by the end of the first half of CY2026, lending clarity into the assessed $50B China market.

Nevertheless, while the H200's initial shipments to China projected for February 2026 was arguably the key news when it was announced just earlier this week, the most significant feature of this week has got to be Jensen Huang's quick closure of the Groq licensing deal. Based on the reported deal dynamics:

  • Groq's IP or intellectual property can be accessed by Nvidia.

  • Groq can stay as an "independent company."
  • Its cloud business "is not part of the transaction."
  • Nvidia will now have Groq founder and CEO Jonathan Ross, Sunny Madra (Groq's president) and also other members in its senior management to "help (Nvidia) advance and scale the licensed technology." In other words, a classic "acqui-hire" deal, so to speak.

Nvidia & Groq Could Transform AI Training And Inferencing

Let's face it. Groq was valued at $6.9B "in a financing (round) just a few months ago." So, the deal price appears undoubtedly to be high, but arguably a highly strategic one that Nvidia is determined to make it happen and believes it has the wherewithal to extract substantive value from it. Hence, it's a palpable upgrade and one that Groq management could be hard-pressed not to seal the deal quickly, since the company lowered its revenue target from $2B to $500M as reported in July 2025. Yet, the company's technology has also been somewhat validated by third party benchmarks (see above), corroborating the confidence placed in the startup by Jensen Huang as he reportedly informed Nvidia employees that the company intends to integrate their tech.

I think it's crystal clear that Nvidia is using its dominant market power and leadership to take control of its destiny by partnering with firms that may threaten its future position (who knows, right?). However, the company also needs to navigate such deals carefully and avoid falling foul to antitrust restrictions that could compel regulators from seeing eye to eye with Nvidia management.

Hence, I thought the non-exclusive licensing deal appears to have done just that. Not only does Groq still retains control over its technology partnerships (non-exclusive licensing), it also expects to continue operating as an "independent company." However, by having the access to the startup's tech this early before it has a chance to establish itself firmly in the chip ecosystem, it affords Nvidia an amazing and timely opportunity to internalize the tech, improving Nvidia's offerings in inference, especially on the "low latency" side, while being highly cost efficient, bolstering Nvidia's capabilities to compete in the increasingly pivotal inferencing growth pillar.

Well, it's not as if the company's chips aren't good enough for inference. I think we can generally agree that Nvidia's merchant GPUs are highly flexible, and are designed to incorporate a wide array of needs, workloads, and requirements from different developers (augmented by its indispensable CUDA libraries); thus earning its plaudits across its customers and adopters.

However, that doesn't mean that determined competitors in the custom space (such as Google (GOOGL) (GOOG)) couldn't reconfigure their strategy and roadmap to compete more effectively, while rewiring their software ecosystem to increasingly making them more palatable for a wider assortment of keen developers who recognize the increasingly capable TPUs, most recently validated by Anthropic's expanded order, and also Meta's reported TPU deployment in 2027.

Nevertheless, the "compute crunch" that has also affected Google's internal AI workloads and requirements could be the "silver lining" that Nvidia needs to try and delay that occurrence, and I believe Jensen Huang sees the opportunity to reshape Nvidia's competitive edge in this space. I believe there will be deeper insights to be shared moving forward as we get more chances to analyze how Nvidia intends to improve its stack with Groq's promising tech, and how it could also elevate its capabilities as compared to its determined and well-resourced arch rivals in the custom space.

For a start, even with the $20B deal that will easily set back any firm without Nvidia's massive free cash flow printing machine, I believe we can all generally agree that Jensen Huang is putting Nvidia's riches to good use. With the firm's FCF expected to scale above the $200B mark by FY2028, I don't expect such breathtaking deals to stop.

The very fact that this AI gold rush is clearly boosting Nvidia's firepower directly should already be regarded a "foregone conclusion" The value extraction by Jensen Huang and his team as the world's foremost AI arms dealer has conferred it such immense financial capabilities to continue doing big deals in its bid to subsume up and coming rivals that could upset Nvidia's competitive moat in the long run.

And unless you think that Nvidia's cash flow prowess could somehow be reversed by Broadcom's (AVGO) or Google's preeminence with its TPU ecosystem, I believe even if Google were to snag a high single digit market share away from Nvidia's entrenched mastery (and that's a big if), it will still take several years to pan out, while assuming "ceteris paribus" for Jensen Huang (not plausible). And I guess he concocted this Christmas eve deal to show us that while the rest of his rivals could be taking a well-deserved break in this holiday season, he's out there consummating deals to defeat even their most ardent efforts to stymie him, and ensure that Nvidia's ultimate destiny remains solely in Nvidia's own hands.

Buy Or Sell NVDA Stock?

Nevertheless, NVDA still fell into an unusual bear market from its October 2025 highs, but saw investors helping the stock consolidate over the past few weeks. Interestingly, the stock has started to regain buying momentum last week, clearing the path for this week's optimism to help elevate the bullish narrative yet again for the Jensen Huang led company.

NVDA's forward earnings multiple of just over 27x is even below the Magnificent Seven's 28.2x metric, showing the level of ambivalence that investors are parlaying into the company's narrative. Yet, that hasn't deterred these dip buyers from seizing the opportunity to regain control of the bullish bias that has defined NVDA's price action for the most part of 2025. In other words, while the current downside appears to be the most pronounced since the April 2025 downside, it does seem like buying fervor hasn't deserted the Jensen Huang and team. And with the chess moves that the King of AI is making against its competitors right now, I believe the moment for a valuation upgrade is upon us, as we head into the closing week of 2025, and welcome what could be another pivotal year for Nvidia for 2026.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Golden Cross And Record Earnings: A Bullish Setup For Chubb
data.description:
Insurance stocks have had their bouts of underperformance in 2025. Unlike previous years, in which ample alpha was seen in home, auto, and life insurance equities, this year has been tougher. Indeed, the SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (KIE) is up less than 10% YTD, dividends included. That lags the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) by precisely 10 percentage points.

One of the largest domestic insurers is Chubb Ltd (CB). I was bullish on the now $123 billion market cap Financials-sector component back in January 2024. Shares are up 35% since then, though that actually lags the SPX a bit.

Today, I reiterate a buy rating. It’s no longer a deep value play, but the earnings growth trajectory appears solid, while the low-teens P/E is attractive.

CB Outperforming KIE YTD

Back in October, Chubb reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $7.49 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $6.15. Strong underwriting totals, along with lower-than-expected catastrophe losses, were tailwinds. Recall that Q3 2024 featured a slew of natural disasters—this year’s July through September period was more tranquil.

Indeed, it was a period of record earnings, raised guidance, and overall decent operational performance. Core operating income was +29% YoY, with EPS up 31% from Q3 2024. Contributions from all regions and segments underscored the firm's diversified growth strategy. The management team guided ROE growth to 14%+, along with noting shareholder-friendly activities on the earnings call.

Shares rallied 2.7% in the session that followed, its best earnings reaction going back to July of 2023, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services. Looking ahead to the January 27 Q4 report, the options market prices in a small 2.9% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. The next dividend is slated to be paid on March 16, while implied volatility on CB shares is very low at just 14%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the bottom-line beat was not only helped by fewer claim payouts, but also by favorable reserve developments. Its Global Property & Casualty segment boasted light loss experiences, though expenses were a tad high in that segment, along with a higher overall tax rate compared to the same period a year earlier.

Of course, insurance is a unique industry, whereby the “cost of goods sold” is only known years after policies are underwritten. Still, underwriting performance was solid, with a favorable 81.5% combined ratio (which is like the inverse of a profit margin). North American Commercial and Personal contributed significantly to the bottom-line beat, with light losses in homeowners and auto.

Premium growth was not all that great, though, and that trend will be worth watching in 2026, particularly in the upcoming report due out in late January. For shareholders, Chubb was active on the stock buyback front, and it’s likely that kept up through the end of 2025. Speaking of Q4, there were reports that Chubb may purchase AIG (AIG), but Chubb denied the rumors on December 10, and speculation has turned quiet since then.

Finally, the company’s balance sheet remains in good shape, with total capital of $89.5 billion, shareholders’ equity of $71.9 billion, net loss reserves of $68.2 billion, and a high-quality $166.0 billion investment portfolio, 81% of which is in fixed-maturity securities with average A/A credit quality and a duration of nearly five years. With a quality balance sheet, it’s not out of the question to see Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) increase its Chubb stake.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to continue to hum along, potentially climbing above $25 in non-GAAP EPS in the out year. This steady profit trend is what makes me optimistic that an above-historical-average P/E multiple is warranted. Overall, the bottom line should rise at a mid-high single-digit rate over the years ahead.

All the while, the sellside has turned quite upbeat on the earnings profile—there have been a high 22 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days with zero downgrades. Finally, profitability trends are about the best you’ll find in the insurance industry.

Chubb: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to what I figured almost two years ago. If we assume normalized operating EPS of $26.25 (the current FY 2026 consensus) and apply a 13x multiple, shares should trade near $341.

That makes CB about 8% undervalued—not a tremendous discount, but reasonable for this steady grower. A 13x P/E is also a PEG ratio of just 1.4x to 1.5x based on prevailing earnings growth trends—quite reasonable.

Chubb: Close to Long-Term Average Valuation Multiples

Key risks for Chubb include increased competition, particularly amid softening property insurance pricing and Excess & Surplus (E&S) line premiums. Given the industry backdrop, there could be downside risk to the earnings growth trajectory—listen up for clues on the Q4 call on that front.

I’d also like to hear more from the management team on its reserve—there were few details there within the Q3 report. Of course, lower market interest rates would be a negative to the bottom line.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, January 27 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still priced attractively in my view, CB’s technical situation is particularly sanguine. Notice in the chart below that shares recently broke out to fresh all-time highs, above $310. The measured move upside price target based on the rally is about $350-$355 based on the depth of the October 2024 to January 2025 decline.

I also like that a golden cross pattern occurred just a few weeks ago, in which the short-term 50-day moving average crossed above the long-term 200dma.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It is also at a new high, helping to confirm the price breakout. Now, with a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample support even if we see CB venture back below the $300 to $307 zone.

CB: Bullish Upside Breakout Targets $350+, Golden Cross

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on Chubb. I see the stalwart insurance stock as attractive on valuation, while the technicals are quite bullish ahead of the Q4 report due out in January.

data.title: Retirement Income And 10%+ Yields: No, They Aren't Mutually Exclusive
data.description:
Usually, investing for retirement income comes with a condition that anything that is put into the portfolio generates reliable, sustainable and stress-free cash flows. This is because you really do not want to assume greater stress than what you had experienced, while building up the nest egg.

The consequences might be painful. Just imagine a situation when you have all planned out, your portfolio current income streams nicely cover the living expenses, you have already been out of the labor market for 10 years and suddenly, one day at market open you realize that a great chunk of your portfolio cash flows are gone. So, either you lower your living standard or re-enter the labor market to build up your capital until the necessary critical mass is achieved. Alternatively, you could start dipping into your portfolio asset base to fund the shortfall between income and expenses. But I do not know how sustainable and smart this kind of approach is.

So, in this context, chasing 10%+ yields would mean that you are simply increasing the probability of that "nightmare scenario" happening. The fundamental relationship in the market (investing) is simple: the higher the yield, the higher the risk.

Given the current interest rate setting, I would say that the sweet spot yield zone for durable income investors is between 5% to 7%. In this territory we can find such gems as Realty Income (O), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and British American Tobacco (BTI) - all with 20+ years of consistent dividend payments.

In the 10%+ zone we have XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT), FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO), Eagle Point Credit (ECC) and Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), which have managed to somehow destroy shareholder value even under an upwards-trending market environment.

The thing with quite many high-yielding products is that they in one way or another use leverage to make their offering/yields more attractive.

Charlie Munger had a great quote on this:

There is no such thing as a 100% sure thing when investing. Thus, the use of leverage is dangerous. A string of wonderful numbers times zero will always equal zero.

However, let me say that even with these risks in mind, I have still managed to inject a decent load of 10%+ yielding products in my core retirement income portfolio. And, importantly, these investments have not weakened my defense or sacrificed the income durability aspect.

In the article below I will elaborate on three key asset classes, where I see that such opportunities could be found.

CEFs

The first asset class is closed end funds, which are tilted towards fixed income securities (not equity). Most CEFs come with a layer of external leverage, which is not fine if the underlying holding base is tied to risky assets.

However, provided that the CEF portfolio investments are inherently stable and generate consist income across different market cycles, then adding smart external leverage could indeed bring the yield into 10%+ zone.

In the chart below, we can see the historical distribution profile of the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), which currently offers ~14.9% in monthly distributions:

Since inception the CEF has generated more than 220% in total returns, while keeping the dividend stable (the period includes different interest rate regimes, COVID-19, oil futures going negative, etc.). The same applies to the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO), which is also a diversified fixed income CEF that uses short-term leverage to stimulate its yield profile. Speaking of PIMCO CEFs and their leverage, it is worth keeping in mind that the borrowings are sourced through reverse REPOs, which cost less as the base rates decline (i.e., positive for PDI and PDO dividend coverage).

BDCs

Business development company (BIZD) asset class is another interesting playing field for scooping up investments picks that yield close to or above 10%. Just as many CEFs and especially PIMCO instruments, BDCs are effectively fixed income products. BDCs lend money to, usually, core middle market businesses, which are too small to access public debt markets and require more tailored financing than what traditional banks can offer (e.g., for M&A, LBO or some other transformational processes).

On the surface, BDCs seem risky because of such words as private credit, leveraged buyouts, not acceptable for banks, etc.

However, the thing is that BDC managers conduct robust origination and underwriting work, attach strict covenants to their loan investment businesses and venture into such conditions, where their loans sit on top of the capital structure (senior secured first-lien) and are backed by private equity sponsors, which have an incentive to step in with fresh equity if things go south (just to keep their investments alive).

Let's take a look at how two - one of the oldest - BDCs have performed over the years, Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Ares Capital (ARCC):

These businesses have even outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY). MAIN has never cut the dividend, and ARCC had to take one cut (of around 18%) in the GFC period, which still resulted in a 10%+ yield.

In my view, durable retirement income investors can find some suitable BDC gems that offer enticing dividends, which are underpinned by resilient portfolios and robust credit statistics. The key for accessing sustainable BDC dividends and stable NAV is to avoid BDC players, which struggle with non-accruals. The dispersion among BDC managers is quite wide:

For example, Fidus Investment (FDUS), Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) are great cases to consider by prudent cash flow investors.

Covered call ETFs

Covered call ETFs are relatively new to the income investing game, especially at the extent and the opportunity set that we see now in the market. The entire logic behind covered call ETF structures is to collect option premiums that could be distributed in high-yielding dividends. They are designed for income-oriented investors.

But the key question is for what kind of income investors?

The short answer is that no retirement income-oriented investor should go so far as to build his or her portfolio around covered call ETFs. I would say that the absolute maximum is 15% of the total exposure, and only then if certain conditions are met.

The main issues are the following:

  • dividends are inherently changing as they depend on the implied volatility of an underlying against which the call options are written.
  • if a steep selloff happens and the market (underlying index) remains depressed there for longer than one or two months (which are typical maturities of written options), then chances are quite big that the income profile will get structurally impaired.

For me, the first is not that huge constraint because in most cases covered call ETFs offer yield that exceed 10%. Having yield volatility in the 9% to 13% range is not that bad, especially if the overall exposure to covered call ETFs is not that pronounced.

The second aspect is, however, something that should be mitigated. So, the key is to avoid two things happening 1) deep correction and 2) no immediate (1 to 2-month) recovery after the correction has happened.

To manage this risk, the only realistic approach I see is to avoid cyclical and overvalued indices that come in the covered call ETF wrapper. I would include the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) and the S&P 500 (SPY) in this bucket. Hence, such popular covered call ETF vehicles as the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), the Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) and the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) should be eliminated from truly durable income investors' radars. In my portfolio, I have included QQQI just to have some flavor of Mag-7 in the mix (but the position is rather small).

In my view, a better way for prudent income investors to capture sizeable cash flows from covered call ETFs is to explore ETFs that are based on less cyclical and more stable indices. The NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI), the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) and the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) the immediate examples that come to my mind (midstream, gold and real estate focus, respectively).

data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: Popular 10%+ Yields Getting Risky
data.description:
I generally prefer investing in high-yielding stocks because I do not need much in the way of growth to still generate attractive total returns. Additionally, since dividends are generally more dependable than earnings growth, high-yield stocks that are backed by high-quality business models and balance sheets tend to be much easier to value than high-flying, high-growth stocks. Therefore, I can make more intelligent buy and sell decisions and overlay an opportunity to the capital recycling program to go along with my value investing to generate superior risk-adjusted total returns over time.

That being said, just because a stock is popular, has an investment-grade business model, has an impressive dividend growth track record, and pays out a high yield does not mean that its payout is sustainable. For example, the late NextEra Energy Partners (XIFR), Algonquin Power & Utilities (AQN), AT&T (T), 3M (MMM), and many other dividend growth stocks that had impressive dividend growth track records and had what appeared to be pretty solid underlying businesses ultimately had to slash their payouts. In this article, I will detail two more high-yielding dividend stocks with strong dividend growth track records that appear to be at risk of imminent dividend cuts.

Blackstone Secured Lending Under The Microscope

The first one I’m going to talk about is Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which is an externally managed BDCs (BIZD) whose asset manager is Blackstone (BX). This gives it immediate credibility as a high-quality BDC, since BX is the world’s largest alternative asset manager with well over $1 trillion in assets under management, as well as a very large private credit business, which gives it scale, deal flow, underwriting and workout expertise, and access to capital.

Not only that, but its portfolio is very defensively positioned and is performing exceptionally well, with 98% of its portfolio invested in first-lien senior secured loans, of which about 98.9% are sponsor-backed. It has a relatively conservative average loan-to-value of about 50%, with an interest coverage ratio of about two times, and non-accruals of just 0.1%. As such, it is one of the highest-quality BDCs out there, alongside others like Ares Capital Corp (ARCC) and Main Street Capital (MAIN).

Additionally, since going public, it has grown its base dividend, while also paying out some supplemental dividends along the way, which has earned it credibility with the market as being an attractive income machine, especially given that its dividend yield has been around or even above 10% most of the time.

The company has also been putting up pretty strong numbers recently in terms of net investment income per share. In its most recent quarter, it saw its net investment income per share increase from $0.77 per share to $0.82 per share, which has created some cushion for its $0.77 quarterly dividend. What that amounts to is a 1.06 times coverage ratio of its base dividend, and it also has $1.89 of spillover income per share that gives it additional flexibility to sustain its dividend.

That being said, there are several signals that the dividend could be at risk. First of all, its leverage is already near the top end of its target range of 1.25 times, as it sits at 1.22 times. Not only that, but it has significant downside exposure to floating-rate interest rates, since 98.6% of its investments are in floating-rate debt. Therefore, whenever the Fed cuts rates, it has an outsized impact on reducing its net investment income per share.

On top of that, it has a significant amount of debt coming due in 2026, all of which is at low fixed interest rates. For example, it has $700 million of debt coming due in September 2026 at a 2.75% interest rate, and another $800 million of debt coming due in January 2026 at a 3.625% interest rate. It will very likely have to refinance that debt at a much higher interest rate, and this will also be followed by $650 million worth of debt maturing in early 2027 at a 2.125% interest rate, and then in 2028, it has an additional $650 million worth of fixed interest rate debt maturing at a 2.85% interest rate.

Therefore, for the foreseeable future, it is going to have a steady wave of fixed interest rate debt maturing that will likely have to be refinanced at materially higher interest rates, which will combine with declining short-term interest rates to squeeze net investment income per share, without having the flexibility on the balance sheet side of things to increase leverage to offset it.

Management also conceded that they could have to cut the dividend at some point by stating on their latest earnings call that they plan to

keep looking at the base dividend in light of where rates and earnings are headed and still make sure it stays competitive and sustainable.

Therefore, while the dividend appears safe for the immediate future, as refinancings kick in over the next year, and certainly over the next two years, combined with the expected continual couple of rate cuts, BXSL may very likely be forced to cut its payout. Investors should keep that in mind when allocating capital to these shares.

LyondellBasell’s Dividend Under Pressure

The next popular high-yielding dividend stock that I’m going to discuss today is LyondellBasell Industries (LYB). LYB is a global chemicals and plastics producer that is facing major macro headwinds due to overcapacity, especially in China, weak global demand, and persistent cost inflation, all of which are leading to margin compression for the company. This has resulted in a steady stream of analyst downgrades, including from BMO Capital recently, as well as growing concerns about the sustainability of its dividend, which has caused its stock price to plunge.

The good news is that the company continues to have an investment-grade credit rating at BBB and is still generating a lot of cash flow and remains highly profitable. In fact, in its third quarter, it generated $938 million of operating cash flow, $835 million of EBITDA, and paid out $443 million in dividends. It has also implemented aggressive cost cuts and cash management strategies, such as reducing capex materially and implementing a cash improvement plan, while also raising its revolver covenant limit to 4.5 times leverage.

However, given that its dividend yield has risen well above 10%, it appears that the market lacks much confidence in the sustainability of the dividend, even though it has an impressive dividend growth track record and even hiked the dividend by over 3% earlier this year, while also paying out a large special dividend a few years ago.

The ultimate issue is that this downcycle has lasted for an extended period of time, and, overall, dividends have exceeded free cash flow recently, thereby forcing the company to reduce its cash balance and increase net leverage in order to support its payout. While LYB has been able to sustain this for a while due to its very strong balance sheet and prudent cash management, ultimately, it cannot make cash appear out of thin air. At a certain point, if industry conditions do not improve soon, management will likely be forced to cut the dividend.

In particular, management has emphasized that they plan to support the dividend for as long as possible due to their commitment to it and desire to maintain their dividend track record, but their desire to maintain their investment-grade credit rating comes first. Once the credit rating agencies signal that they are on the verge of a downgrade, they are very likely to have to slash the dividend.

Therefore, this is a very speculative play as far as the dividend is concerned. While a separate case can be made that the business remains well-positioned over the long term, management has executed very well, and LYB has outperformed peers like Dow Inc. (DOW) recently; the valuation argument is a separate matter.

Investors who are in the stock primarily for the high yield need to keep in mind that a dividend cut is highly likely.

Investor Takeaway

While BXSL and LYB have very strong track records, investment-grade credit ratings, and skilled management teams that have done well by shareholders over time and remain competitively positioned for the long term, both of their dividends are on shaky ground. BXSL is facing headwinds from having to refinance fixed-rate debt coming due in the coming months and years at materially higher interest rates, while at the same time, the yields on its investments are declining due to Federal Reserve rate cuts. This is likely going to pressure its already razor-thin dividend coverage.

Meanwhile, LYB is facing a prolonged industry downturn and is running out of runway on its balance sheet to support the dividend through the trough. Therefore, it would not shock me at all to see one or both of these stocks slash their payouts in the next 6 to 12 months.

That said, both do present reasonably attractive valuations today. For investors who are willing to weather the potential volatility and uncertainty that come with a dividend cut and instead see these as quality businesses on sale that are trading at attractive prices for a long-term hold, they could certainly make sense as investments right now. This is why we hold a small position in LYB and have BXSL high on our watch list right now at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Ventas Is Hitting On All Cylinders
data.description:
Growth stocks come in all shapes, sizes, and forms. That’s why investors shouldn’t get ‘FOMO’ when it comes to not owning popular ones like NVIDIA (NVDA) or Google (GOOG). Moreover, there are plenty of names that offer growth potential and income to boot, thereby giving investors a cut of the profits. This can make it easier for ‘buy and hold’ investors through getting an income stream along the way up.

This brings me to Ventas (VTR), which I last covered in April, highlighting its growing SHOP (senior housing operating) portfolio and resumption of meaningful dividend growth. VTR has since delivered a strong 23% total return since my last piece. At the current price of $79.62, VTR trades near its 52-week high of $81.89 with a forward P/FFO of 22.9, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit VTR including recent business results, and discuss why despite the recent run-up, it’s a well-positioned growth stock at present, so let’s get started!

Why VTR?

Ventas is a member of the S&P 500 (SPY) and is a leading REIT that owns a diversified portfolio of senior housing communities, outpatient medical and research facilities across the U.S., Canada, and the UK. At present, VTR has 1,400 properties in its portfolio including more than 850 senior housing communities. This positions the company to benefit from an expanding cohort of older adults seeking quality living arrangements.

Notably, VTR has strategically grown its exposure to SHOP properties over the past 4 years, from 30% of total NOI to 49% at present. This includes converting properties from a triple-net lease structure to SHOP in markets with strong demand. This enables VTR to capture more of the operating profits (due to its participation in operating expenses) compared to a triple-net lease. At the same time, VTR has transitioned low occupancy communities to the right operators while acquiring high-performing properties with upside potential.

VTR continues to demonstrate strong operating fundamentals during Q3 2025. This is reflected by normalized FFO per share growing by 10% YoY to $0.88, total NOI growth of 20%, and same-store cash NOI rising by 8% over the prior year period. This was driven by strong performance in the SHOP portfolio, which saw robust SSNOI growth of 16% YoY.

Notably, the SHOP segment saw RevPOR (revenue per occupied room) growth of 4.7% YoY, due to dynamic pricing strategies that balanced rates with occupancy. Average occupancy grew by a solid 270 basis points YoY, led by a 340-basis point increase in VTR’s U.S. communities. This was driven by broad demand growth and strong performance from key operators like Sunrise and Atria.

At the same time Outpatient Medical and Research segment saw respectable SS cash NOI growth of 3.7%. This was driven by growth in outpatient medical while the research segment (representing 8% of total NOI) saw softness due to lower rents in the life science space.

Encouragingly, management raised full-year FFO per share growth guidance to 9% and SS cash NOI growth to 7.5% on the back of strong third quarter results. This would equate to top-tier performance relative to peers like Welltower (WELL) if achieved. As shown below, robust results in the SHOP segment has caused management to raise full-year SHOP SSNOI growth guidance from 13.5% in February to 15.0% in October.

VTR also has avenues to growth externally, as it closed $2.2 billion of private-pay senior housing investments in the first 10 months of the year. This was spread across 20 transactions for 6,200 units. Management has noted that these investments were acquired typically below replacement costs, thereby enhancing long-term return potential. Long-term growth is supported by the growing 80+ age population, which is expected to rise by 28% over the next 5 years. As shown below, this is expected to drive consecutive increases in occupancy through 2030.

Moreover, VTR expects to manage this wave of demand with the help of the internally developed Ventas OI platform, which delivers pricing insights among other things, as noted during the last earnings call:

Our portfolio strategy executed through our Ventas OI platform is centered on what we call the right market, right asset, right operator approach, is a disciplined framework that ensures every investment we make in every partnership that we pursue, enhances long-term value creation.

We've spent years building a platform that's ready for this wave of demand in senior housing. We now have sophisticated data analytics and the ability to deliver those insights directly to our operators through our Ventas OI platform. We've enhanced our CapEx management, optimized dynamic pricing and developed a broader platform capabilities needed to effectively drive performance and support 40 operators managing our communities and that number continues to grow.

Importantly, VTR carries a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $4.1 billion of liquidity and a safe net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 5.3x, which is lower by 1.0x over the past 12 months. This supports the 2.4% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 55% payout ratio. Notably, VTR resumed dividend raises earlier this year with a 6.7% increase after keeping it flat post-cut in 2020.

Admittedly, VTR is no longer in value range at the current price of $79.62 with forward P/FFO of 22.9. As shown below, this sits above its historical P/FFO of 14.7.

However, VTR’s valuation is far more favorable compared to that of peer Welltower, which has a forward P/FFO of 35.7. I believe VTR is deserving of its premium over historical valuation due to robust growth tailwinds. This is supported by analyst estimates for 9% to 13% FFO/share growth annually over the next 3 years. As such, I believe the current valuation solidly puts VTR in the ‘growth at a reasonable price’ category, as it evolves from a value stock to a growth stock.

Risks to VTR include concentration risks as it relates to high exposure to the SHOP segment. This property segment comes with more potential volatility (versus triple net leases) since VTR participates in the operating expenses of the underlying properties. As such, changes near-term demand could have an outsized effect on VTR’s bottom line. Labor inflation and staffing constraints could pressure property level margins and cap occupancy rates. VTR also has risks from operator-specific headwinds, as that could lead to lower growth while operators are transitioned.

Investor Takeaway

Ventas has transitioned from a slow-growth, post-pandemic recovery story into a credible growth REIT. This is driven by strong execution in its SHOP portfolio, improving occupancy, disciplined acquisitions below replacement cost. Its strong balance sheet supports external growth.

While shares now trade well above historical valuation metrics, the premium appears justified by accelerating FFO and NOI growth and favorable long-term demographic tailwinds. This is also supported by management’s ability to capture operating upside through data-driven pricing and operator optimization.

Compared to peer Welltower, VTR offers a more balanced mix of growth and valuation, making it a reasonable ‘GARP’ stock for long-term investors. As such, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on the stock.

data.title: The Value Traps Of The REIT Sector
data.description:
Over the past year, I have written many articles highlighting how cheap REITs (VNQ) have gotten.

They have suffered a multi-year bear market, with share prices declining to lower levels, even as their cash flows kept on rising, and as a result, they are now even cheaper than during the Great Financial Crisis by some measures:

But just because a REIT is cheap does not mean that it is a good investment opportunity.

There are plenty of REITs that are heavily discounted, and yet, I am not interested in them whatsoever.

That's because they face severe headwinds that are likely to turn them into value traps over time.

Here are 5 good examples of that:

Hotel REITs

Hotel REITs are popular among a lot of bargain-hunting investors because they commonly offer much lower Price-to-FFO multiples than REITs operating in other property sectors:

Hotel REITs Broader REIT Market
P/FFO 8-12x 12-16x

But before you rush to buy them, you should consider that there are good reasons for these lower multiples:

  • First, they are much more cyclical. Hotels don't have long-term leases to protect their cash flows. As discretionary income drops, people travel less, and hotels immediately feel the pain.
  • Second, they require significant CapEx over time to remain attractive. The hotel business is very competitive, and unless you keep reinvesting in your properties, you quickly lose your guests.
  • Third, the competition from Airbnb (ABNB) and other similar platforms is ever-growing, leading to more supply of units available for travelers, and reducing the pricing power of hotels.
  • Finally, the value of hotel brands is declining as a result of Booking websites, which make it very easy for guests to review and compare hotel offerings.

Taking all of this into account, I think that this is one of the least attractive property sectors to invest in. Yes, valuation multiples appear relatively low, but this is because they are based on peak-year cash flows and are typically not adjusted for the CapEx. The long-term prospects are also uncertain due to the growing disruption caused by Airbnb and other Booking sites.

Hotel REITs were the least profitable sector of the REIT market over the past decades, and I think that their future performance will only suffer further:

With that said, if I was going to invest in a hotel REIT, I would stick with one that has a strong balance sheet to withstand recessions, and focuses on the luxury segment that can better resist the competition from Airbnb. Host Hotel (HST) would be my pick, but it is not cheap enough for me to invest in today.

Hong Kong REITs

REIT or REIT-like entities today exist in over 40 different countries, and one market that consistently gets a lot of attention from value investors is Hong Kong REITs, or HK-REITs in short.

A lot of them, including Link REIT (LKREF), Fortune REIT (FRIVF), and Sunlight REIT (SHTRF), trade at extreme discounts to NAV, reaching up to 70% in some cases. The pitch then becomes that these REITs allow you to buy a stake in prime Hong Kong real estate for pennies on the dollar:

But if it sounds too good to be true, it probably is, and in this case, there are good reasons for these massive discounts.

  • First, the NAVs are based on very low cap rates, and many of these REITs use quite a bit of leverage. Therefore, despite trading at large discounts to NAV, the actual implied cap rates of these REITs are not all that high. If market cap rates are in the high 3s, these REITs may trade in the mid 5s. That's a discount, but it is not as cheap as it may first seem.
  • Second, these REITs own primarily offices, which are out of favor. Often, these REITs also do not own the land beneath their buildings. Instead, they lease them on a long-term basis and only own the improvements.
  • Third, there are significant conflicts of interest, with many REITs being externally managed (more on this later), and/or managed as family businesses with little regards for the interest of other shareholders.
  • Finally, and perhaps most importantly, China's grip on Hong Kong is ever growing, and this is pushing a lot of foreign companies and expats to relocate to Singapore or elsewhere. It reduces the demand for office space and may lead to cap rate expansion over time.

These REITs have always seemed undervalued, and yet, they have only gotten cheaper over time. They are the perfect definition of a value trap.

Externally Managed REITs

I mentioned earlier that a lot of HK-REITs are externally managed, which leads to conflicts of interest. But it is not just HK-REITs. There are still many REITs, even in the U.S., that remain externally managed.

What this means is that the management of the REIT is outsourced to an external company, which then takes care of the management in exchange for fee income.

To give you an example: RMR (RMR) manages Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHR) and earns management fees from it.

The issue is that this model often pushes the management to take on too much debt and issue a lot of shares in order to grow the size of the portfolio, as this then leads to higher fee income for them.

The fee income is generally a function of the volume of assets under management - a clear conflict of interest!

This is well-reflected in the performance of all the RMR-managed REITs: Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHR), Service Properties Trust (SVC), Office Properties Income Trust (OPITQ), and Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT).

They have all faced significant dilution from equity raises and suffered massive value destruction over time due to overleverage.

But it is not just RMR.

Most externally managed REITs have done far worse than their internally managed peers over time.

They always seem cheap, and yet, they keep underperforming. They are value traps.

Office REITs

Office REITs are now also increasingly attracting the attention of value investors, and it is easy to understand why.

Class A assets enjoy strong demand as office tenants consolidate their office footprint towards the best assets. Despite that, all office REITs are out of favor and trade at low valuations.

But there's a catch. Actually, two of them.

  • First, I expect a lot of vacant Class B office buildings to change hands over the years, with new owners coming in with much lower bases, which will then allow them to heavily reinvest in these properties to make them more competitive with Class A assets. It will lead to greater price competition and put pressure on rents and occupancy rates. So far, the Class A stock has mostly been insulated from the competition of vacant Class B buildings, but I expect this to change over the coming years. There is a record 20%+ vacancy rate in the office space, and I expect this to remain a drag on the sector for a long time to come.
  • Second, I expect the AI revolution to have a major negative impact on office demand in the near term. Companies don't know how many employees they will need in just a few years from now, and therefore, it would be reckless to sign a new 10-15-year office lease right now. Tenants are likely to delay major office leasing decisions and require more flexible office lease terms. If lease terms are shortened, it may become more difficult to finance these buildings, also lead to higher cap rates. I think that we are just now starting to see this impact on the office sector.

Therefore, I think that the low valuations of REITs like SL Green (SLG) and BXP (BXP) are justified, and I fear that they will turn into value traps over the coming years.

Closing Note

The reason why I have become good at identifying value traps is that I have unfortunately invested in a lot of them in the past.

These are some examples, but they are not the only ones either.

This is a sector in which you need to be very selective if you want to succeed, as there are vast differences from one REIT to another.

At High Yield Landlord, we only invest in about one REIT out of ten on average, and that's how we have managed to outperform the broader REIT sector over time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 17 Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Buys From 30 Of 73 November Graham Value All-Stars (GVAS)
data.description:
Foreword

About Large Cap Value

“A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during their stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1s are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios.” -YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, seventeen of the thirty “safer” lowest-priced dogs of the GASV are buyable.

December found Noah Holdings (NOAH), Alliance Resource (ARLP), Westlake Chemical (WLKP), IRSA Inversiones (IRS), Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX LP (MPLX), Global Ship (GSL), Altria (MO), Verizon (VZ), SunCoke Energy (SXC), DHT Holdings (DHT), Kilroy Realty (KHC), Ennis (EBF), FinVolution (FINV), AT&T (T), JD.com (JD), and Banco Bilbao (BBVA) on the buy-list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced December GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all fourteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 36.21% To 98.4% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By December, 2026

Eight of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 80% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target-prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to December, 2026 were:

Hafnia (HAFN) was projected to net $983.97 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 743% opposite the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 50.81% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 0.45% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten November GASV by dividend and price as of market close 12/22/2025.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield showed less than ideal status. The prices of all ten are below the projected annual dividends from $1k invested. Yes, all ten are IDEAL. But not all are “safer.” Several are paying dividends with borrowed money.

Costs per $1 of Dividend

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. That condition was reached by forty of the sixty-four highest yield December GASV.

The 50 forward looking dividend reporting stocks in the December GASV Costs Per $1 of dividend ranged from $7.85 for TORM Plc, to $25.35 for EOG Resources Inc.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Fourteen of Sixty-four Dividend Paying GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash. Fourteen GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy. They are paying dividends from borrowed money

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own it. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead: dread the Fed, the toll of trading terms, wars, tacos, and uncertainty.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed for Ten Top Yield GASV “Fair-Priced” For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from that top chart show that all ten are more than 50% away from having a share price greater than divided paid from $1k invested..

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 25.9% To 88.32% Top Ten GASV Upsides To December 2025; (32) No Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 21.77% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of December 22, 2026

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 12/22/25 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors. Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care, Industrials Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 57.25% Vs. (35) 47.01% Net Gains by All Ten Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 21.77% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The very lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Hafnia Ltd showed top analyst-estimated gains of 98.40%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for December were Hafnia Ltd, Noah Holdings, Golub Capital, RMR Group, Irsa Inversiones, with prices ranging from $5.31 to $15.35.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of December 22 were: Energy Transfer; Westlake; TORM; Ares; & Alliance Resource, with prices ranging from $16.30 to $23.35.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the football and are-year buzz euphoria dies and the feckless Tech-fueled market inflation and dithering presidential deals gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of the few hot stocks and memes, look for more GASV to become fair-priced… slow and sure, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 17 Ideal of 30 ‘Safer' December GASV Dogs

Late-December 2025, 30 of the 70 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 12/22/25 data showed 17 of those were fair-priced and IDEAL to buy at this time.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced “Break-Even” Price Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend GASV conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those ten ideal dogs were: Noah Holdings, Alliance Resource, Westlake, IRSA, Energy Transfer, MPLX, Global Ship, Altria, Verizon, and SunCoke.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

All ten share prices of these “safer” survivors are below the dividend payouts from $1k invested. The top chart reveals all ten prices would need to rise more than 10% to exceed the dividend payout from $1k invested.

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as “good” as they can get.
